definitions.definition: Deciding in advance what to do, how to do it, when to do it, and who is to do it. It bridges the gap from where we are to where we want to go.
The end points that management seeks to achieve. They must be measurable and provide direction for all managerial activities.
A comprehensive plan to achieve organisational objectives, considering the competitive environment and available resources.
A general statement that guides decision-making. Allows some flexibility. Example: 'We promote from within.'
A series of routine steps to carry out activities in chronological order. More detailed than policy, less flexible.
A prescribed way of performing a specific task. Even more detailed than procedure — deals with one step only.
A specific statement that tells what must or must not be done. No flexibility or discretion. Example: 'No smoking in the office.'
A detailed plan for a specific project — includes objectives, policies, procedures, rules, budgets, and time schedule.
A plan expressed in numerical terms showing expected results — revenue, expenditure, units to be produced.

points.a: Objectives, Strategy, Policy, Procedure, Method, Rule

points.b: No flexibility — must be followed exactly
Demands a specific action or restriction

scenarios.scenario: A company decides to increase its market share by 20% in the next fiscal year.
scenarios.principle: End point that management seeks to achieve

scenarios.scenario: A firm announces 'We will not sell to retailers who also stock competitor products.'
scenarios.principle: General guideline that allows some discretion in interpretation

scenarios.scenario: A sign in the factory says 'Helmets must be worn in the production area at all times.'
scenarios.principle: Specific statement — no flexibility or discretion

scenarios.scenario: To process a customer refund: Step 1 → Verify receipt. Step 2 → Check return window. Step 3 → Approve and credit.
scenarios.principle: Routine steps carried out in chronological order

scenarios.scenario: A company plans to launch a new product — setting objectives, allocating budget, hiring team, fixing deadlines.
scenarios.principle: Detailed plan for a specific project with multiple components

scenarios.scenario: The sales department prepares a forecast: expected revenue ₹50 lakh, expenses ₹30 lakh, profit ₹20 lakh.
Despite having a solid plan, the company fails because the market changed due to sudden government policy.
scenarios.answer: Limitation: Plans may not work in a dynamic environment
scenarios.principle: Future is uncertain — even the best plans may fail

scenarios.scenario: Junior managers feel demotivated because they must strictly follow plans made by top management with no room for their own ideas.
scenarios.principle: Middle and lower levels just follow — no scope for own thinking

scenarios.scenario: A firm spends months collecting data and analysing alternatives before finalising its expansion plan.
scenarios.answer: Limitation: Planning is time-consuming and costly
scenarios.principle: Scientific planning involves research, forecasting, and expertise

scenarios.scenario: Planning sets the target of producing 5,000 units. Controlling measures actual output and finds only 4,200 were produced.
scenarios.answer: Planning and Controlling are inseparable twins
scenarios.principle: Planning sets standards; Controlling measures performance against them
